caveats: a: OEWS is for 532100 and does not isolate 532120 or measure task exposure.
a: Ryder and U-Haul are large-company examples and may have more automation than LMM operators.

rho: Results may differ sharply between multi-year truck fleets and branch-based consumer RV or trailer rentals.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Truck, Utility Trailer, and RV (Recreational Vehicle) Rental and Leasing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The code mixes short-duration transaction revenue from consumer and commercial rentals with multi-year commercial leases and bundled maintenance, fuel, compliance, and digital fleet services. Customers pay time- and usage-based rental charges or recurring lease and service fees.
lens.deviation_from_default: none; the full default population is retained despite the code's short-term consumer and long-term commercial submodels, with uncertainty represented in the primitive ranges
lens.excluded_activities: Light-duty passenger-truck and SUV rental or leasing classified in 53211; rentals with drivers; dealer sales; captive internal fleets; passive financing vehicles; and firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms renting or leasing trucks, truck tractors, buses, semi-trailers, utility trailers, or recreational vehicles without drivers to external commercial or consumer customers, including repeat short-term rental and recurring full-service lease relationships.

caveats: OEWS is for 532100 and does not isolate 532120 or measure task exposure.
Ryder and U-Haul are large-company examples and may have more automation than LMM operators.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit payroll-by-occupation data and time-and-motion studies split between truck leasing, truck/trailer rental, and RV rental, with task hours for reservation, dispatch, contract, maintenance, billing, service, and physical work, would materially refine a.
a.rationale: The closest OEWS group is led by counter/rental clerks but also has large physical cohorts of vehicle cleaners, diesel mechanics, truck drivers, motor-vehicle operators, and automotive technicians. AI exposure is concentrated in reservations, contract intake, customer service, billing, maintenance triage, compliance, scheduling, and fleet analytics; cleaning, repair, vehicle movement, inspection, and yard work remain largely physical. The range is a wage-weighted task judgment rather than a measured exposure statistic.

d5.bridge: U-Haul's self-moving equipment rental revenue rose from $3.625 billion in fiscal 2024 to $3.726 billion in fiscal 2025, while Ryder reported long-term commercial leasing alongside weaker commercial rental conditions in 2025. These large-company nominal observations are translated to a modest 1.08 five-year constant-price service-quantity base rather than extrapolated mechanically. Large network expansion and price changes likely overstate LMM industry quantity growth, so the range includes a 10% decline.
caveats: U-Haul mixes truck and trailer rentals with other businesses, and revenue changes include price and mix.
Ryder is a large commercial fleet company and its conditions may not represent consumer RV or trailer operators or LMM firms.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of Census Service Annual Survey or Economic Census constant-price receipts and transaction or fleet-on-rent counts for 532120, split by truck lease, truck/trailer rental, and RV rental and by firm size, would replace the proxy.
d5.rationale: Freight, household moves, project work, seasonal capacity, and businesses avoiding fleet capital support continuing rental and leasing demand. Cyclicality, remote-work migration normalization, freight weakness, customer ownership, and utilization technology constrain the five-year outlook.

caveats: Eligibility within the estimated LMM population is not observed.
The frozen n value depends on a round 30% EBITDA-margin assumption that may misclassify capital-intensive firms.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level registry linking 532120 establishments to external contracts, service revenue, ownership/control, fleet assets, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure the eligible share across the truck, trailer, and RV submodels.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on operating rental or leasing services to external customers, so most LMM operating firms fit the lens. Ineligible cases include passive fleet-owning or finance vehicles, captive units, dealer-led businesses whose primary economics are sales, and entities without transferable operations.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $7,590.6M / sectoral output (T30) $33,462.2M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.3000 (margins.json[532]: ESTIMATE: rental & leasing is asset-heavy with no clean Damodaran bucket (REITs 43.79% overstates operating rental); 30% chosen as a round mid-point before deriving results); revenue/employee $417,601 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 532120); class <5: 1,173 firms, avg revenue/firm $660,261 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $198,078 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 228 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,101,316 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $630,395 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 126 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,116,897 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,535,069 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,325,532 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,197,660 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 80 firms, avg revenue/firm $63,348,850 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,004,655 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $428,473,464 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $128,542,039 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 250

caveats: No source directly measures operator substitution for the full 532120 basket.
Peer-to-peer RV and trailer platforms could reduce traditional operator share faster than commercial truck leasing.
o.change_evidence: Buyer cohorts tracking cancellations, peer-to-peer substitution, direct ownership, software-only fleet management, autonomous handoff, and retained operator contracts by rental/lease submodel would materially move o.
o.rationale: Customers may self-book and software can automate dispatch and fleet administration, but an accountable operator must still own or control the asset, ensure availability, maintain and inspect it, manage safety and compliance, handle physical pickup or delivery, and resolve damage and roadside events. Software is therefore unlikely to replace most operator-required quantity within five years.

caveats: The cited filings describe contract structures but not the retained share of automation benefits.
Pricing power differs materially between bespoke commercial leases and price-transparent consumer rentals.
q.change_evidence: Contract and renewal samples across full-service truck leases and short-term truck, trailer, and RV rentals showing billing units, escalation, open-book terms, repricing, gainsharing, credits, technology cost, and realized post-automation margin would materially refine q.
q.rationale: Multi-year commercial leases can preserve workflow savings within an existing term, while short-term rentals can retain savings through dynamic prices if competition does not force immediate pass-through. Renewal tenders, transparent time-and-mileage pricing, customer sharing, lower fees, service-level credits, and technology costs reduce retention.

caveats: Digital platforms and structured contracts show readiness but do not establish realized AI labor substitution.
Results may differ sharply between multi-year truck fleets and branch-based consumer RV or trailer rentals.
rho.change_evidence: LMM operator deployment cohorts reporting baseline and post-AI handling time, automated reservation and maintenance decisions, exception and override rates, damage-claim accuracy, staffing, and realized hours released over at least twelve months would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: Lease and rental workflows already generate reservations, contracts, vehicle, usage, maintenance, and payment data suitable for AI-assisted processing. Implementation is constrained by fragmented branch systems, safety and maintenance accountability, customer-specific equipment, damage disputes, fraud, physical handoffs, and the need for human overrides.

caveats: BizBuySell's 31 reported Equipment Rental and Dealers sales in 2025 combine industries and are mostly below the target EBITDA band.
Fleet disposals, lease-book transfers, franchise changes, and internal reorganizations are excluded from qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A deduplicated five-year set of closed 532120 operating-company control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell brokers, and commercial fleet lenders, divided by a verified eligible-firm panel, would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: The broad equipment-rental marketplace shows completed deals, supporting the existence of a transfer market, but it supplies neither a 532120 denominator nor an LMM-specific five-year hazard. Fleet financing, asset appraisals, lender consents, customer concentration, and owner succession create both sale incentives and closing friction.

principal_driver: Physical asset accountability makes demand hard to replace entirely while data-rich fleet workflows offer targeted automation potential.
principal_weakness: Capital intensity and physical maintenance limit labor leverage, and the code's mixed customer and contract models widen every commercial estimate.
